tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates guideline-concordant symptomatic therapy for Raynaud (calcium channel blocker) and life-saving therapy when renal crisis is suspected/confirmed (ACE inhibitor), while ensuring required safety checks before prescribing.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "scleroderma" and links it to "Raynaud" as a common vascular manifestation (cold-/stress-induced color change, pain/numbness).

Explains the treatment plan and explicitly mentions both a "calcium channel blocker" (for Raynaud) and an "ACE inhibitor" (for suspected/confirmed scleroderma renal crisis or hypertension in systemic sclerosis).

Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; if a relevant allergy is present (e.g., ACE inhibitor allergy/angioedema history, dihydropyridine CCB allergy), avoids that agent and documents an alternative plan.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing: avoids ACE inhibitors in pregnancy; uses caution/avoids in prior ACE-inhibitor–associated angioedema, bilateral renal artery stenosis, or severe hyperkalemia; assesses hypotension/bradycardia/heart failure considerations for calcium channel blockers.

Considers drug–drug interactions before prescribing (non-critical): identifies major interaction risks (e.g., ACE inhibitor with potassium supplements/spironolactone/trimethoprim increasing hyperkalemia risk; NSAIDs potentially worsening renal function with ACE inhibitor) and mitigates when relevant.

Provides safety-net advice: urgent evaluation for signs of renal crisis (new severe headache/visual changes, rapidly rising BP, reduced urine output), digital ischemia (ulceration, severe pain, black discoloration), or cardiopulmonary symptoms (new/worsening dyspnea, chest pain, syncope).

Uses patient-centered communication (checks understanding, invites questions) and avoids overpromising; clarifies that systemic sclerosis is chronic and management focuses on controlling symptoms and preventing organ complications.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose systemic sclerosis and start first-line Raynaud therapy with a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker; include ACE inhibitor initiation when renal crisis is suspected/confirmed or with new/worsening hypertension suggestive of renal crisis, after required safety checks.
must_collect: Raynaud symptoms (cold/stress triggers, color change, pain/numbness)
Blood pressure trend or current BP (screen for hypertension/renal crisis)
Current meds/supplements (NSAIDs, potassium supplements, diuretics, TMP-SMX, lithium)
Renal risk symptoms (headache, visual changes, oliguria) or recent creatinine rise if known

Optionally assess interaction risks (NSAIDs, potassium-raising agents, TMP-SMX) and counsel/adjust
Prescribe a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker (e.g., amlodipine) for Raynaud; advise non-pharmacologic measures (keep warm, avoid smoking/vasoconstrictors)
If renal crisis suspected/confirmed or new/worsening hypertension in systemic sclerosis: initiate ACE inhibitor (e.g., captopril) and give urgent follow-up/ER precautions
Provide safety-net warnings and brief follow-up plan (BP monitoring, renal function monitoring) in narrative

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 26-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start with: “I keep losing my hearing, and I’m getting more clumsy and unsteady on my feet.” Volunteer: hearing has worsened over years (worse in noisy places), you veer/stumble when walking, and you drop things/feel clumsy with your hands. If asked about other symptoms, disclose: poor vision in dim light, occasional spinning/“on a boat” sensation with quick turns, intermittent numb/tingly feet, fatigue with longer walks, and on/off ringing in ears. Do not volunteer hidden details unless specifically asked about night driving, low-light vision, glare, injuries, or balance in the dark; then admit bumping into objects in low light, glare sensitivity, slow dark adaptation, unnoticed foot cuts/burns, worse balance with eyes closed (e.g., shower), and avoiding night driving. Express misconceptions: you wondered about Meniere’s, MS, or screen time/new glasses. Withhold sexual/erection problems unless directly asked about sexual function; then be embarrassed but honest. If clinician shows empathy, relax, thank them, and share worries about insurance coverage and job/diagnosis impacts.

confounders: Family history of retinitis pigmentosa (raises consideration of non-syndromic RP)

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifying PHARC syndrome frames expectations (progressive multisystem involvement) and drives appropriate counseling, genetic confirmation, multidisciplinary care, and avoidance of inappropriate acute treatments.

nl_assertions: States the working diagnosis explicitly as PHARC syndrome and avoids mislabeling as isolated retinitis pigmentosa, Charcot-Marie-Tooth, MS, or Usher syndrome without acknowledging PHARC as unifying.

Explains that PHARC syndrome is a rare inherited genetic condition (typically autosomal recessive) and that genetic testing (e.g., ABHD12 sequencing/panel) is used to confirm the diagnosis and support family counseling.
Explains treatment is supportive care for PHARC syndrome (no proven disease-modifying therapy), emphasizing symptom management and multidisciplinary follow-up (vision, hearing, neurology, rehab).
Uses clear, patient-friendly language, checks understanding (teach-back or equivalent), and addresses prognosis uncertainty and variability without overpromising cure.

If the agent proposes any medication despite L1 scope, it performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical safety check) and additionally considers drug interaction and contraindication checks before prescribing (non-critical).
Recommends appropriate referrals conceptually (ophthalmology/retina, audiology/ENT, neurology, genetics) even if not executing referral tools for an L1 diagnosis-only task.
Avoids unsafe or unsupported claims (e.g., steroids/immunotherapy as definitive treatment) and avoids advising unproven supplements as curative.

minimal_paths.description: Recognize the characteristic multisystem pattern and document PHARC syndrome; counsel on genetic confirmation and supportive care.
must_collect: progressive vision loss/nyctalopia or retinitis pigmentosa history
peripheral neuropathy symptoms (numbness, tingling, weakness) or gait imbalance/ataxia
steps: Synthesize symptoms as a single unifying rare genetic syndrome consistent with PHARC (polyneuropathy, hearing loss, ataxia, retinitis pigmentosa, cataract).
Explain diagnosis in plain language and explicitly name PHARC syndrome.
Recommend genetic testing to confirm (ABHD12) and discuss implications for family members (carrier status, recurrence risk).
Explain treatment approach as supportive care (vision/hearing aids, rehab/physio, fall prevention, symptom control) and need for multidisciplinary monitoring.
Record diagnosis: PHARC syndrome.

acceptable_variants.description: Records a probable/working diagnosis of PHARC syndrome pending genetic confirmation, after briefly acknowledging key differentials and why PHARC best fits.
steps: Identify pattern suggesting PHARC and mention key differentials (Usher syndrome, Charcot-Marie-Tooth with retinal dystrophy, mitochondrial disorders) while stating PHARC is most consistent given the full constellation.
Recommend confirmatory genetic testing (ABHD12) and genetic counseling.
Explain supportive care focus and outline multidisciplinary follow-up.
Record diagnosis as 'Suspected PHARC syndrome (pending genetic confirmation)'.

acceptable_variants.description: Emphasizes counseling and next steps (genetics referral, supportive services) while still completing the single required outcome tool for L1.
steps: Explain PHARC syndrome and expected organ systems involved; check understanding and patient concerns.
Discuss genetic testing process, possible results, and family implications.
Outline supportive care and monitoring plan (ophthalmology, audiology, neurology, PT/OT).
Record diagnosis: PHARC syndrome.

She gets very upset by loud noises or changes in routine and can have intense meltdowns

general_concerns: Thinks atypical Rett syndrome can be triggered or worsened mainly by vaccines or a past viral infection rather than being a genetic neurodevelopmental condition.
Believes that once her sister is in her late 20s, the condition should "plateau" and swallowing/breathing problems are unlikely to get worse.

Assumes that increased hand-wringing and agitation are always behavioral and should be addressed primarily with stricter routines rather than looking for pain, reflux, constipation, seizures, or sleep issues.
Worries that physical therapy might "wear out" her sister’s muscles and accelerate decline, so she has been limiting activity.
Thinks seizures would always look like full-body convulsions and may miss subtle events (staring spells, sudden drops, brief stiffening).
Believes genetic results are only useful for family planning and won’t change day-to-day management, so follow-up feels unnecessary.
economic_concerns: Assumes insurance will not cover repeat evaluations (neurology, swallow study, sleep study) and delays scheduling to avoid surprise bills.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old woman, anxious but clear, speaking as the primary caregiver for your younger sister. Open with: “She’s been losing skills and having these weird hand movements, and we’re worried something is really wrong.” Early in the visit, volunteer: loss of words/pointing over the last year, repetitive hand-wringing/rubbing (worse with excitement/stress), increased unsteadiness/clumsiness, and very disrupted sleep.

If asked about history, share: normal early development then plateau/regression around preschool; awake breathing spells (breath-holding/hyperventilation); brief staring episodes; sensory sensitivity and meltdowns; declining hand use for feeding/play; constipation needing stool softeners.

Do not mention unless specifically prompted: teeth grinding, cold hands/feet, chewing/pocketing food, reflux after meals, slowed growth/picky eating, better eye communication after warming up.

Resist/feel embarrassed about stool smearing/accidents and self-hitting/hand-biting; only admit if asked gently. Mention “shaking spells in sleep” only if asked about seizures/sleep.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s autism, vaccines/toxins, ADHD, or anxiety. Emphasize cost fears and reluctance to schedule tests/DME/services. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more willing to share sensitive details.

MECP2 genetic testing.value: Pathogenic variant detected (heterozygous MECP2 c.808C>T, p.R270X)

medications: polyethylene glycol 17 g once daily as needed for constipation
comorbidities: epilepsy (focal seizures with occasional generalized tonic-clonic seizures)
autonomic dysfunction (baseline bradycardia and low blood pressure)
confounders: autism spectrum disorder diagnosis in childhood with limited verbal communication
cerebral palsy label given after early motor delay, complicating attribution of spasticity/gait abnormalities
iron-deficiency anemia and low BMI from restricted intake/dysphagia mimicking primary fatigue/weakness
SSRI (sertraline) and baseline Rett-associated QTc prolongation increasing risk of arrhythmia and syncope
intermittent hyperventilation/breath-holding spells mistaken for panic attacks or seizure activity
recurrent aspiration events causing episodic cough and low-grade symptoms that can be misattributed to anxiety or GERD

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 22-year-old man. Be cooperative but vague and a little unsure; answer briefly unless the clinician asks follow-up. Start by saying: “My mom says my head is getting bigger and I’ve been getting more headaches.” Early on, volunteer: hats don’t fit like before; headaches a few times a week in the front, sometimes worse in the morning; you get tired easily and want naps; you’ve always learned slower and need help with forms/money.

If asked about associated symptoms, mention: nausea and a couple vomiting episodes with headaches; occasional blurry vision; more clumsy/tripping; loud snoring and sometimes waking up choking; a couple episodes of staring and not answering for about a minute, then confusion.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: light sensitivity, waking to pee at night, worse memory for tasks, increased irritability.

Resist/deny bedwetting at first (embarrassed); only admit if asked directly and reassured.

Offer misconceptions: screen time, “sinuses/full face,” energy drinks causing headaches. If clinician is empathetic, relax, say you’re scared about costs, losing your job, and insurance/therapy coverage, and ask if tests/specialists will be expensive.

confounders: Risperidone-associated weight gain and metabolic syndrome risk (can mimic endocrine causes of cognitive/behavioral change)

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check), screens contraindications to methotrexate and steroids (contraindication_check) including pregnancy/trying to conceive, liver disease/alcohol use disorder, significant renal impairment, active infection, chronic hepatitis/TB risk, and evaluates steroid risks (uncontrolled diabetes, active infection, severe osteoporosis, peptic ulcer).

Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (drug_interaction_check) with attention to methotrexate interactions (e.g., trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, high-dose NSAIDs, certain antibiotics) and anticoagulants where relevant; adjusts plan or provides warnings if present.

Avoids unsafe prescribing: does not prescribe methotrexate if pregnancy is possible without contraception plan, or if clear contraindication is identified; does not continue steroids as long-term monotherapy for RA.
